coredata.dc:title: Multi-functional nitrogen self-doped graphene quantum dots for boosting the photovoltaic performance of BHJ solar cells
coredata.dc:creator: Moon, Byung Joon
Jang, Dawon
Yi, Yeonjin
Lee, Hyunbok
Kim, Sang Jin
Oh, Yelin
Lee, Sang Hyun
Park, Min
Lee, Sungho
Bae, Sukang
coredata.dc:description: The tunable heteroatom doping density in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can provide unique opportunities for advanced electrochemical and opto-electronic applications with control of intrinsic properties that allow exploiting new phenomena. Herein, we report a facile one-step synthesis of the nitrogen-doped high-crystallinity GQDs (nGQDs) from poly-acrylonitrile (PAN)-based CFs using the solvo-thermal cutting method. Interestingly, the optical properties of nGQDs can be simply controlled by varying the heat treatment temperature of the CFs with different N contents. We also conduct an in-depth study on the optical properties of nGQDs according to the variation of N atom density that can be readily modulated by controlling the graphitization temperature of CFs, via both experimental and computational analyses. The synthesized nGQDs are blended with PEDOT:PSS as an anodic buffer layer to induce efficient hole extraction and energy-down-shift in organic photovoltaic (OPV) devices that provide an enhanced power conversion efficiency (PCE) from 7.5% to 8.5%. Because of the wide absorption band, high carrier extraction, and non-toxicity, these nGQDs are demonstrated to be excellent probes for high-performance opto-electronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PAN fiber
Hydrothermal cutting
N-doped graphene quantum dot
Photoluminescence
Hole transport layer

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst vitae

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) A representative scheme for the preparation of nGQDs from PAN-based CFs by chemical cutting and exfoliation process. (b) TEM images (left, inset: FFT image) of nGQDs and their size distribution (right). (c) AFM height images of nGQDs (left) and their height profile (right).

(a) Elemental compositions of nGQDs prepared from PAN-based CFs under the same experimental condition. (b) Chemical compositions of N-containing groups in two types of nGQDs obtained from N 1s XPS spectra. (c) NEXAFS and (d) Raman spectra of two different types of nGQDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption and (b) PLE spectra of nGQDs. (c) Normalized PL spectra of nGQDs (λex: 400nm). (d) TRPL spectra of nGQDs monitored at different emission wavelengths (λem: 450nm and 550nm, λex: 375nm).

(a) Energy level diagram of the selected N-doped graphene QDs showing the PL transition possibility from the DFT calculations. The calculated LUMO+2, LUMO+1 and LUMO levels (blue) and the HOMO level (red) are displayed with the s-orbital contribution percentage. The energy gaps between the unoccupied level having s-orbital characteristics higher than 2% and the HOMO level are shown in bold type. (b) PL spectra of nGQDs (λex: 400nm). (c) Possible PL transition scheme for nGQDs.

(a) J-V characteristics of the conventional PTB7:PC71BM solar cells under AM 1.5G filtered illumination at an incident intensity of 100mW/cm2. (b) EQE spectra of the cPSCs with various HTLs. (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of the pristine PEDOT:PSS, PDQD12 and PDQD28 films on ITO substrates. (d) Time-resolved decay curves of nGQD12 and nGQD12/PC71BM on ITO substrates.

(a) Dark J-V curves for hole-only devices incorporating PTB7:PC71BM (1:1.5w/w). (b) Simplified energy diagram at the anode side of the cPSCs with various HTLs. (c) Energy level diagrams near the ITO/HTLs in the presence of an interfacial dipole with negative or positive magnitude.

head.ce:title: Multi-functional nitrogen self-doped graphene quantum dots for boosting the photovoltaic performance of BHJ solar cells

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The tunable heteroatom doping density in graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can provide unique opportunities for advanced electrochemical and opto-electronic applications with control of intrinsic properties that allow exploiting new phenomena. Herein, we report a facile one-step synthesis of the nitrogen-doped high-crystallinity GQDs (nGQDs) from poly-acrylonitrile (PAN)-based CFs using the solvo-thermal cutting method. Interestingly, the optical properties of nGQDs can be simply controlled by varying the heat treatment temperature of the CFs with different N contents. We also conduct an in-depth study on the optical properties of nGQDs according to the variation of N atom density that can be readily modulated by controlling the graphitization temperature of CFs, via both experimental and computational analyses. The synthesized nGQDs are blended with PEDOT:PSS as an anodic buffer layer to induce efficient hole extraction and energy-down-shift in organic photovoltaic (OPV) devices that provide an enhanced power conversion efficiency (PCE) from 7.5% to 8.5%. Because of the wide absorption band, high carrier extraction, and non-toxicity, these nGQDs are demonstrated to be excellent probes for high-performance opto-electronic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have focused on the synthesis and functionalization process in the last few years and have become a rising star in the family of graphene because of their outstanding properties (e.g., a highly tunable photoluminescence (PL) property, good biocompatibility, a remarkable UV-blocking property, high photostability and a unique spin property) and wide range of applications in bio-imaging, photocatalysts, ion detection, electrochemical luminescence, photo-detectors, photovoltaic devices and optical sensors [1–9]. To date, various approaches have been developed for the preparation of GQDs, involving acidic oxidation, hydrothermal method, microwave synthesis, electrochemical approaches, oxygen plasma etching, catalyzed cage-opening, pyrolysis and exfoliation methods [10–16]. Among them, reducing the size of carbonaceous materials (e.g., graphite, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphite oxides and pitch fibers) down to ~ 10nm via chemical oxidation and the cutting process is one of the most widely known and easiest methods to acquire highly crystalline GQDs that are mainly composed of a graphitic carbon structure (e.g., sp2-hybridized crystal structure) with a variety of O-containing functional groups (i.e., hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH) and epoxy groups (C-O-C)), as well as to modulate their band structural, electronic, magnetic, optical and electrochemical properties caused by quantum confinement and edge effects [10,14,17–19]. In addition to geometrical engineering, the heteroatom-doping process in graphitic carbon materials is an effective route to manipulate their intrinsic properties for applications in optical and electronic devices [20-22].

Recently, many studies on heteroatom-doped GQDs have been reported for the incorporation of nitrogen (N)-containing groups into a graphitic carbon structure via electrochemical and post-chemical approaches [23–26]. Li et al. reported that in situ N-doping in the graphitic structure can lead to the modulation of chemical and electronic characteristics of the GQDs caused by the high electronic affinity of N atoms [23]. Additionally, to control the optical properties, Tetsuka et al. synthesized edge-terminated N-doped GQDs by using a primary amine that enables the electronic structures to be adjusted systemically through the effectual orbital resonance of amine moieties with a graphene core [24]. Because of N-doping or amino-group functionalization, optical and electrical properties of GQDs substantially changed, such as the appearance of up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) and improvement of catalytic activities [26,27].

Unfortunately, in spite of these developments, the density of N-containing groups in the π-conjugated framework (graphitic structure) still remains to be done with regard to the control of their distributions (doping uniformity or concentration) and bonding configurations (chemical bonding structure). For instance, the doping concentration of N-doped GQDs is difficult to precisely control to less than 5at% [25,26,28]. Additionally, their locations are mostly limited to edge or defect sites of GQDs [24,29–31]. However, based on recent reports, substitutional N-doping (i.e., quaternary-N) is more effective than the interstitial counterparts because it is able to significantly modulate the band structure of GQDs and possibly make unique and positive potentials [26,32,33]. However, such incorporation of N atoms in the graphene lattice through the post-treatment process is likely to disrupt sp2 hybridization of C atoms and even to induce structural defects in GQDs, possibly hindering their chemical stability as well as electrical (or optical) properties, and thus, substitutional N-doped GQDs with high crystallinity and outstanding intrinsic properties have not been acquired [34,35].

Here, we report a facile one-step synthesis of the substitutional N-doped GQDs (nGQDs) from poly-acrylonitrile (PAN)-based carbon fibers (CFs) for the first time ( Fig. 1a). In contrast to pitch-based CFs, PAN-based CFs have N-oxides (Nox), pyridinic-N (Npyri), pyridonic-N (Npydo) and a relatively high content of quaternary-N (Nq) that is widely known to improve the electrical conductivity of carbon-based nano-materials (CNMs) without destroying sp2-hybridized graphitic structures [36–38], because the N atoms in the C≡N group of PAN are not completely removed during carbonization. Moreover, we expect that a less dense structure of PAN-based CFs (ρ PAN≈1.8gcm−3 and ρ pitch≈2.1gcm−3) has favorable effects on reaction solvent diffusion and penetration into CFs [39]. Additionally, we conduct an in-depth study on the optical properties of nGQDs according to the variation of N atom density via both experimental and computational analyses. Finally, nGQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS composites are introduced into organic photovoltaic (OPV) devices as an efficient hole transport and photo-response material, leading to a 14.5% enhancement in PCE compared to cells with a pristine PEDOT:PSS layer.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of nGQDs, as shown in Fig. 1b, reveal that they are circular and highly crystalline and have a narrow size distribution that is between 3 and 5nm with an average diameter of approximately 4.29 (nGQD12) and 4.40nm (nGQD28), as depicted in Fig. 1b. The inset in Fig. 1b displays lattice parameters of 0.34nm, representing the (002) lattice fringe of graphene that is similar to the reported values of carbon-based QDs prepared by different methods [40]. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images of nGQDs in Fig. 1c reveal that their heights are mostly less than 2.0nm, indicating that nGQDs typically consist of a few layers of graphene (less than 4 layers).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out to determine the composition of nGQDs. In Fig. S1, carbon (C) 1s, oxygen (O) 1s and nitrogen (N) 1s peaks, as three major elements of nGQDs, are observed. As the heat treatment temperature of CFs increases, interestingly, the atomic ratio of the C atoms increases, whereas those of the N and O atoms decrease as shown in Fig. 2a. In accordance with XPS results, the FT-IR results in Fig. S3 show that there are significant differences in the intensities of the peaks corresponding to the O- or N-related functional groups, such as the O-H (or N-H) stretching vibration peak at 3700–3300cm−1 (broad), N-OH stretching vibration peak at 3300–3150cm−1, C˭O stretching vibration peak at ~1745cm−1 and C-O stretching vibration peak at ~1100cm−1 [41-43].

Another interesting finding is that the contents of O atoms in nGQDs seem to decrease with the O concentration of PAN-derived CFs, although the degree of oxidation is difficult to control because of the thermal oxidation and acid-assisted cutting process (H2SO4:HNO3=3:1, by volume). As far as we are concerned, oxygen-related functional groups in common GQDs synthesized by chemical oxidation and cutting of various CNMs can be controlled only by extra processing steps, such as the reduction and edge-functionalization process using chemical and electrochemical methods [24,44]. However, in the case of our approach, without additional processes, the amount of O atoms can be systemically modulated by the heat treatment temperature of CFs.

As mentioned above, the high-resolution N 1s spectra can be de-convoluted into four signals for Npydi, Npydo, Nox and Nq, appearing at 398.2, 400.5, 405.0, and 401.1eV, respectively (Fig. S2). Among different N-doping configurations, Nq is well-known to elevate the electrical conductivity of graphitic structures because it aids in maintaining the electron delocalization effects without destroying sp2-hybridized graphitic structures [45]. Npydi contains sp2-hybridized nitrogen atoms located at the edges (Npydi-1) or in the vacant sites (Npydi-2) of the graphitic structure. Npydi-1 maintains sp2-hybridized graphitic structures because extra electron pairs lie in the molecule facing outward; thus, the electrons can be comparatively well-delocalized [46,47]. However, in the case of Npydi-2, relaxation around the vacancy can be considered to proceed via a Jahn-Teller distortion; thus, the hexagonal graphitic structures are distorted [48]. Additionally, Npydo and Nox cannot contribute to the restoration of the graphitic structure because N or C atoms include dangling bonds that unavoidably disrupt recovery of the hexagonal graphitic structure [49]. Ironically, although the N content of nGQD12 is nearly twice that of nGQD28, the Nq contents of each nGQD remain almost unchanged while the sums of the Npydi, Npydo, and Nox contents are significantly varied. It is well known that a low heat treatment temperature (<700°C) of PAN fibers produces substantial amounts of various gases, such as H2O, CO2, CO, HCN, and NH3, and the evolution of N2 begins at a high heat treatment temperature (>700°C), and thus, CFs heat-treated at a higher temperature contain a reduced amount of N atoms [50]. Therefore, the XPS results provide that Nq, the most stable form in the N-containing chemical structures of nGQDs, is not changed, whereas other nitrogen components are removed from PAN-based CFs and nGQDs during extreme heat treatment at 2880°C and hydrothermal-assisted synthesis of QDs, respectively.

The Raman spectra in Fig. 2c present the intrinsic characteristics of sp2 carbon with a disordered structure. These spectra have two distinct peaks that correspond to the D and G bands at ~1390 and ~1610cm−1, respectively. As the graphitization temperature of PAN-based scrap CFs increases, the intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) decreases from 1.00 (nGQD12) to 0.92 (nGQD28), while the D and G peak is red-shifted from 1385cm−1 (D peak position of nGQD12) to 1368cm−1 (D peak position of nGQD28) and from 1607cm−1 (G peak position for nGQD12) to 1581cm−1 (G peak position for nGQD28), respectively. Additionally, compared to nGQD28, the nGQD12 G peak has a significantly larger full-width at half-maximum (FWHM). There are several possible mechanisms for the variation of the Raman peaks, including the effects of strain, doping and partial reduction. The substitution of N atoms and vacancy defects in nGQDs that can be initially modulated by controlling the graphitization temperatures of CFs would cause distortion of the lattice and defect pining (Fig. S1) [50]. Additionally, the existence of G peaks at a higher frequency indicates the formation of defects in the graphene lattice resulting from the excessive oxidation of CFs. According to the empirical Tuinstra-Koenig relationship [51], the presence of those defects in nGQDs inevitably influences the sp2 cluster size (La), which increases by approximately 10% (nGQD12: 4.3→nGQD28: 4.8nm) because of the reduction of the zero-D point-like defects and thermal de-oxidation in CFs. Additionally, to further obtain information on the degree of bond hybridization in mixed sp2/sp3 bonded carbon, including the specific bonding configurations of neighboring functional atoms and the graphitization level of nGQDs, we conducted high-resolution in situ synchrotron near-edge X-ray absorption fine structure (NEXAFS) measurements (Fig. 2c). These spectra were acquired at an incidence angle of 54.7° to reduce the effects of sample orientation on the relative intensities of the different resonances. The lowest energy resonance at approximately 284.8eV is ascribed to the transition of C 1s core-level electrons into states of π* symmetry around the M and L points of the graphene Brillouin zone above the Fermi level, while the higher energy peak at approximately 294.4eV is assigned to the excitation of C 1s core electrons into dispersionless states of σ* symmetry [52]. The π* feature is typical of the unsaturated C˭C or graphitic (sp2) bond above and below the plane, involved in the C-N transitions (CK1), whereas the in-plane σ* feature is representative of the saturated C-C bond. Moreover, the three resonances in the intermediate region between the π* and σ* features are observed at ~286.9eV (CK2), ~288.1eV (CK3) and ~289.3eV (CK4), corresponding to functional groups (CK2: C-O, CK3: C˭O and CK4: O-C˭O, respectively) attached at the basal planes and edges of nGQDs [53].

Most remarkably, the relative intensities of the CK2 resonances (CK2/π*), related to the ratio of oxygenated C atoms, show a similar trend as the XPS and FT-IR results, as shown in Figs. S1 and S3. Based on this evidence, we can reconfirm that the oxidation level of nGQDs can be effectively controlled by the graphitization temperature of CFs. Additionally, the relative intensities of σ* resonances (σ*/π*) present an estimate of the relative concentration of sp2 domain configurations in an sp3 matrix consisting of C atoms connected to heteroatom-containing groups. Therefore, in comparison with nGQD12, it is possible that nGQD28 can be composed of more sp2-hybridized C atoms and even tinier amounts of non-graphitic components (i.e., structural defects, vacancies and C atoms connected to O-including functional groups).

As previously mentioned, the optical properties of nGQDs can be tailored by controlling the heat treatment temperature of PAN-based scrap CFs that effectively contain different N contents and substitutions in scalable amounts ( Fig. 3). To explore the optical properties of nGQDs, first, the UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained as shown in Fig. 3a. nGQDs in aqueous solution have three distinct UV–vis absorption peaks at approximately 230nm (π→π* transition), 265nm (n1→π* transition) and 285nm (n2→π* transition). Moreover, compared with nGQD28, nGQD12 has higher absorption in the mid-UV and visible region (approximately 200–700nm) because it consists of many substitutional N atoms as well as N- and O-rich functional groups. Analogously, as shown in Fig. 3b, PL excitation (PLE) spectra also show two electronic transitions at 273–275nm and 330–350nm that can be considered as the two types of transitions triggered by the π→π* excitation and n→π* excitation, respectively. The corresponding PL spectra at pH 8 that are normalized by their maximum intensities to show how the emission spectra are tuned by the N atom contents in nGQDs are presented in Fig. 3c. First, most GQDs are normally known to show excitation wavelength (λex)-dependent PL emission in the 300–400nm range, whereas we observe λex-independent PL emission in our nGQDs (Fig. S5). Although the correlation between λex and PL emission wavelength (λem) is not fully studied, a few reports recently showed that the narrow size distribution and high crystallinity of GQDs can be critical factors behind the λex-independent PL emission [54]. Furthermore, compared with the PL spectrum of nGQD28, that of nGQD12 with the maximum emission wavelength at 544nm is significantly red-shifted as well as has a notably broader full-width at half maximum (FWHM). The difference in the PL characteristics of nGQDs is considered to be as follows. In general, the PL of heteroatom-doped GQDs with a large π-conjugated system originates from the π*→π electron transition (or intrinsic state emission) and is induced by quantum size effects, zigzag edge, and other factors [14]. Moreover, PL characteristics of GQDs are derived from a defect state emission. To be specific, the n1→π electron transition is observed because of the presence of O-containing groups [7]. However, it is widely known that those groups partially lead to non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs and the suppression of intrinsic state emission, although O atoms have a strong electron affinity [30]. In contrast, N atoms (Npyri, Nq and Nox) with many lone pair electrons in the graphitic structure act as effective radiative recombination centers and enable the tuning of the PL emission wavelength [55].

To clarify the PL mechanism discussed above for nGQDs, we performed PL decay experiments at room temperature with the pulsed laser excitation at 375nm, and the results were fitted using a tri-exponential function to calculate the lifetime of nGQDs. The average exciton lifetime (τavr) calculated using the formula τav=(A 1τ1 2+A 2τ2 2+A 3τ3 2)/(A 1τ1+A 2τ2+A 3τ3), where τ1, τ2 and τ3 are the lifetimes (Table S1), is quite different. As shown in Fig. 3d, the PL decay rate of nGQDs becomes slower as the number of N atoms in them increases (e.g., 2.19ns (nGQD28)→2.80ns (nGQD12) for λem=550nm). Additionally, we can observe that the PL lifetimes for nGQDs are longer at the 550nm wavelength compared with those at the 450nm wavelength, indicating that the two emission regions have different electronic origins [56]. It is widely known that heterogeneous electronic structures derived from different dynamic behaviors of chemical states have a critical impact on the emission properties of carbon-based QDs. Therefore, we are able to assert that the deviation of PL lifetime in nGQDs strongly reflects the chemical nature of each nGQD originating from substitutional N atoms as well as N- or O-containing edge functional groups.

To conduct additional investigation on the origin of PL in nGQDs, DFT calculations were performed on pristine (PG) and N-doped graphene QDs (all of the attempted geometries are shown in Fig. S7). According to the quantum mechanical selection rule, only electrons having s- (l=0) or d- (l=2) orbital characteristics can transit to the C 2p (l=1) valence band of aromatic rings, which are associated with the observed peaks in the PL spectra (Δl=±1). The molecular orbital coefficient analysis reveals that the frontier energy levels of PG are solely composed of the p-orbitals, indicating that the PL transition is prohibited. Therefore, we can assume that the observed PL transitions are attributed to the N-doping effect. In DFT calculation results, among various types of N-doped graphene QDs, certain s-orbital contributions to the frontier energy levels are evaluated for Npydi-graphene QDs (NGpydi) and Nox-graphene QDs (NGox), whereas those of Nq-graphene QDs (NGq) and Npydo-graphene QDs (NGpydo) only consist of the p-orbitals. The largest difference among NGpydi, NGox and the others is the planarity. Specifically, in the case of NGpydi, a vacancy distorts the planar structure of PG, causing two adjacent hydrogen (H) atoms to be located out of the graphene QDs plane. Similarly, a hydroxyl group on NGox leads to the non-planar structure in which the H atom of a hydroxyl group is situated out of the PG plane. This structural distortion impedes the electron delocalization on PG; thus, the newly formed localized states with the s-orbital characteristic enable the PL transition. Fig. 4a depicts the energy level diagram of the calculated LUMO+2, LUMO+1, LUMO and HOMO levels with the corresponding s-orbital characteristic percentages. In geom 1 of NGpydi, the calculated s-orbital contributions of the α-electron to the LUMO+2 (−1.30eV), LUMO+1 (−1.88eV) and LUMO levels (−2.18eV) are 1.5%, 2.2% and 0.7%, respectively, and those of the β-electron to LUMO+2 (−1.76eV), LUMO+1 (−2.07eV) and LUMO (−3.08eV) levels are 2.7%, 0.7% and 2.6%, respectively. In geom 2 of NGpydi, the s-orbital contributions of the α-electron to the LUMO+2 (−1.29eV), LUMO+1 (−2.02eV) and LUMO levels (−2.12eV) are evaluated as 1.6%, 1.1% and 0.6%, and those of the β-electron to LUMO+2 (−1.91eV), LUMO+1 (−2.03eV) and LUMO (−2.89eV) levels are 1.2%, 0.6% and 2.9%, respectively. In the case of NGox, the s-orbital contribution to LUMO+1 (−1.46eV) level is 0.9% for the α-electron, while the s-orbital contributions to LUMO+2 (−1.25eV) and LUMO (−2.49eV) levels are 1.2% and 2.0% for the β-electron, respectively. To summarize, the PL transitions would be feasible for several energy gaps ranging from 1.88 to 3.65eV by the 0.6–2.9% s-orbital contributions. On the basis of these DFT calculations, therefore, in terms of the structural characteristics, the PL transition is likely to originate from NGpydi and NGox, whereas the other types of N-doping would only improve the electrical conductivity. The calculation results also show that both the blue-shifted PL peak and the decreased PL intensity at longer wavelengths of nGQD28 would arise from the reduced contents of Npydi and Nox. Although the calculated energy gaps are not identical to the observed PL peak positions resulting from the intrinsic limitations of the DFT method (e.g., an underestimation of the band gap), this model does not deteriorate the underlying physics; thus, the origin of the PL process would be reliably demonstrated as shown in Fig. 4b and c.

Finally, to confirm the potential opto-electronic applications of nGQDs with remarkable optical and electrical properties, we fabricated and characterized conventional polymer solar cells (cPSCs) with the nGQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS interlayer as a hole transport layer (HTL). These HTLs are prepared by adding two types of ethanol based-nGQD solutions into the PEDOT:PSS solution. The presence of O-rich functional groups causes the nGQDs to be soluble in hydrophilic media (e.g., water, ethanol and 2-propanol), and thus, they are well-dispersed in the PEDOT:PSS layer without any noticeable aggregation. Additionally, as shown in Fig. S8, we can observe that average grain sizes for modified PEDOT:PSS layers are somewhat increased because the negatively charged nGQDs caused by lone pair electrons of N atoms tend to combine with positively charged PEDOT polymers. Generally, the grain size of the PEDOT:PSS layer is determined by tight ionic bonds between conductive PEDOT-rich particles and insulated PSS-rich lamellas [57]. However, by the introduction of nGQDs into the PEDOT:PSS layer, the net electric charge of PEDOT-rich particles is somewhat decreased, possibly leading to the reduction of the electrostatic interaction between PEDOT-rich and PSS-rich domains. Therefore, on the basis of previous reports, we are able to anticipate that such an effect is beneficial to the electric conductivity and light-transmittance of HTLs, resulting in significant improvement of PCEs [58].

Fig. 5a shows the J-V curves of the cPSCs fabricated from PTB7:PC71BM blend solutions with different types of HTLs. The control device exhibits a short circuit current density (J SC) of 15.1mA/cm2, an open-circuit voltage (V OC) of 0.74V, a fill factor (FF) of 0.67 and a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 7.5% (Table S2), which are comparable to the values reported in the literature [59]. The 0.5wt% addition of nGQDs into the PEDOT:PSS layer leads to significant enhancements in J SC (15.1mA/cm2→16.6mA/cm2 (nGQD12), 16.0mA/cm2 (nGQD28)). These findings imply that incorporation of nGQDs into the PEDOT:PSS layer is likely to have a great effect on charge generation and transport processes at the interface between the ITO electrode and photo-active layer (PTB7:PC71BM).

To verify the reasons for the considerable changes in photovoltaic performance of cPSCs, especially in J SC, we first acquired the external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectra of devices with different HTL films. As shown in Fig. 5b, the incorporation of nGQDs into the PEDOT:PSS layer causes significant EQE enhancements in the wavelength range from 340<λ<700nm. Theoretically, the EQE is able to be expressed as follows [60]:
(1)

η
CC
where η A is the absorption efficiency, η ED is the exciton diffusion efficiency or the probability of excitons diffusing to the donor-acceptor (D-A) interface, η CT is the charge transfer efficiency at the interface, and η CC is the charge collection efficiency, which is the probability of photogenerated charges collected at the electrodes. Based on this equation, we can expect that the addition of nGQDs to the PEDOT:PSS layer is likely to have a great influence on η A as well as η CT when using the same device configuration.

First, to determine the effects of nGQDs on the photon collection property, we obtained the absorption spectra for three types of HTL, such as the intrinsic PEDOT:PSS layer and two different PEDOT:PSS with nGQDs (PEDOT:PSS+0.5wt% of nGQD12 (denoted as PDQD12) and PEDOT:PSS+0.5wt% of nGQD28 (denoted as PDQD28) layers, on ITO-coated glass substrates. As shown in Fig. 5c, nGQD-incorporated HTL films (PDQD12 and PDQD28) show the additional spectral responses between 300 and 500nm compared with the intrinsic PEDOT:PSS layer. As we expected, PDQD12 has higher light-absorption in the visible region than PDQD28 because of its many substitutional N atoms as well as N- and O-rich edge-functional groups. As shown in Fig. 5d, PL decay lifetimes of nGQD12/PC71BM samples monitored at 500nm are much shorter than that for intrinsic nGQD samples, indicating that the photo-induced excitons are well-dissociated at nGCDs/PC71BM interfaces and efficient electron (or energy) transfers from nGQDs to PC71BM occur in these systems. For more in-depth study, we determined the efficiency of charge pair formation (E) that resulted from light absorbed by the nGQD12 using the following formula [61]:
(2)
E
= 1 −
(
τ
DA
/
τ
D
)
where τD and τDA are the donor (nGQD12) excited state lifetime with and without the acceptor (PC71BM), respectively. Based on this time-resolved data, we can achieve transfer efficiencies of 41% (τD=2458ps and τDA=1437ps, λem: 475nm), representing that nGQDs play an important role as an efficient sub-light harvester and down-energy converter for free charge carrier generation in cPSCs.

Furthermore, as aforementioned, the enhanced J SC can be interpreted as a consequence of the improved charge carrier transport as well as suppressed charge recombination at the interface between the photo-active layer and ITO electrode. Therefore, to determine the charge transfer dynamics in cPSCs with PEDOT:PSS-based HTLs, first, we investigated high binding energy cutoff (E cutoff) regions of different ITO-based anodes as shown in Fig. S12 For the UPS measurement, various types of HTLs were coated onto UV-O3-treated ITO substrates (u-ITO) by spin-casting. With a variation in the chemical composition of nGQDs, the work-function of u-ITO/PDQD12 increases from 4.88eV (u-ITO) to 5.12eV; in contrast, that of u-ITO/PDQD28 decreases to 4.79eV as displayed in Fig. 6b. The work-function of PDQD12 is closer to the HOMO energy level of PTB7 (donor, 5.15eV) than that of PDQD28. It is known that energy level matching is an important factor to reduce the charge collection barrier from the photo-active layer to the electrode [62]. In accordance with UPS results, as shown in Fig. S13, TRPL measurements of the PTB7 show a decrease in the PL lifetime of the photo-active layer from 688ps (w/o PEDOT:PSS layer) to 441 and 472ps in the presence of PDQD12 and PDQD28, respectively. These results directly indicate that nGQDs can act as sub-efficient hole extraction sites in HTL films. Similarly, from the dark J-V characteristics (Fig. S9), leakage currents slightly decrease after incorporation of each nGQDs into the PEDOT:PSS layer. It is widely believed that lower leakage currents imply that the HTL is efficient in the charge transfer processes due to enhancement of the hole extraction efficiency.

Additionally, investigation of E cutoff regions enables us to extract the shift in the vacuum level (E vac), indicating the formation of an interfacial dipole (Δ) that is equal to subtracting the work-function of u-ITO (Φu-ITO) from the difference between the E vac of HELs and Fermi energy level (E F). As illustrated in Fig. 6c, incorporation of nGQD12 into the PEDOT:PSS layer results in a shift toward lower energy, while introduction of nGQD28 leads to a less-pronounced shift toward higher energy. Therefore, by using ITO/PDQD12 as the anode in cPSCs, photo-induced charge carriers at the PDQD12/active layer interface can be more easily transported to the ITO electrode under the favorable influence of its relatively large and positive interface dipole (Δ=0.24eV), resulting in enhanced J SC (15.1mA/cm2→16.6mA/cm2).

To further investigate the dependence of the charge transport property of cPSCs on incorporation of nGQDs into the PEDOT:PSS layer, we fabricated hole-only devices with the PTB7:PC71BM heterogeneous blend system to estimate hole mobility using the Mott-Gurney square law, as follows [59,63]:
(3)
J
SCLC
=
9
8
ε
0
ε
r
μ
(
V
2
L
3
)
where J, L, μ, ε
r , ε
0 , and V are the current density, film thickness of the PTB7:PC71BM layer, carrier (hole or electron) mobility, relative dielectric constant of the transport medium, permittivity of free space (8.85×10–12 Fm−1), and internal voltage in the device, respectively. In addition, the internal voltage is expressed by the following [64]:
(4)
V
=
V
appl
−
V
r
−
V
bi
where V
appl , V
r , and V
bi are the applied voltage to the device, the voltage drop resulting from the contact resistance and series resistance across the electrodes, and the built-in voltage because of the relative work function difference between the two electrodes, respectively. The V
bi can be determined from the transition between the Ohmic region and the SCLC region. As shown in Fig. 6a, the hole mobility (μ h) increases from 1.05×10−4 cm2 V−1 S−1 (intrinsic PEDOT:PSS layer) to 5.42×10−4 cm2 V−1 S−1 (PDQD12). This result indicates that the hole, injected from PTB7, can be easily extracted for the device based on PDQD12. In general, the hole transfer in conjugated-polymer (i.e., P3HT, PCDTBT and PTB7):PC71BM-based solar cells is slower than electron transfer. The incorporation of nGQDs into the PEDOT:PSS interlayer for the purpose of obtaining higher hole mobility results in a good balance between the electron and hole mobilities in devices, resulting in an increase in J SC. Another notable observation is that the value of μ h of the cPSCs with the PDQD28 layer is higher than with the pristine PEDOT:PSS layer. As we know, Nq and Npydi1 are capable of enhancing the electrical conductivity of carbon-based QDs while preserving the sp2-hybridized graphitic structures, and thus, we can understand why incorporation of nGQD28 into the PEDOT:PSS layer in cPSCs can improve the power conversion efficiency (PCE). Therefore, we can conclude that this improvement in the mobility is likely to be partially attributed to the high conductivity of nGQDs.

We report a facile strategy to synthesize nGQDs from PAN-based CFs that can easily regulate the N/C and O/C atomic ratios and N-bonding configurations by altering the heat treatment temperature of CFs. Because of the chemical nature of each CF, as-prepared nGQDs have not only a high degree of optical tunability but also have outstanding crystallinity leading to λex-independent PL emission. Finally, to confirm the possibility of opto-electronic applications using the remarkable optical and electrical properties of nGQDs, we fabricated cPSCs with the nGQD-incorporated PEDOT:PSS interlayer as a multifunctional interfacial layer, resulting in a 14.5% enhancement in PCE compared to cells with a pristine PEDOT:PSS layer. Therefore, we believe that our nGQDs can provide opportunities in a wealth of applications, such as photocatalysts, energy storage and biological labeling, in the immediate future with further study.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) Institutional Program. Also, it was supported by the Graphene Materials/Components Development Project (10044366) and the Industrial Core Technology Development Program (10052760) through the Ministry of Trade, Industry, and Energy (MOTIE), Republic of Korea.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2017.02.013.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supporting information
.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Byung Joon Moon received the B.S degree in Chemical Engineering from Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU) in 2011 and the M.S. degree from Pohang University of Science and Technology (POSTECH), Korea, in 2013. Then, he has worked as a research scientist in Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST). His primary research interests include nano-material synthesis (0-2D) and its opto-electronic applications.

Dawon Jang received her undergraduate degree from Chonbuk National University in 2014. She is currently a Ph.D. course student supervised by Prof. Sungho Lee at Department of Nano Material Engineering, Korea University of Science and Technology (UST). Her research is mainly focused on the manufacturing process of carbon fibers and carbon nanomaterials.

Yeonjin Yi is an Associate Professor in Department of Physics at Yonsei University, Republic of Korea. He received his Ph.D. from Yonsei University in Department of Physics in 2005. After working as a postdoctoral researcher at University of South Florida and as a senior researcher at Korea Research Institute of Standard and Science (KRISS), he joined the Department of Physics, Yonsei University in 2011. His research interest is focused on the interfacial electronic structures of molecular solids and low dimensional materials and related devices.

Hyunbok Lee is an Assistant Professor in Department of Physics at Kangwon National University, Republic of Korea. He received his Ph.D. from Department of Physics at Yonsei University in 2012. He worked in Atomic-scale Surface Science Research Center at Yonsei University (2012–2013) and Department of Polymer Science and Engineering at University of Massachusetts Amherst (2013–2015) as a postdoctoral researcher. After that, he joined the Department of Physics at Kangwon National University in 2015. His research is focused on the electronic structure modification at the interfaces for efficient organic devices.

Sang Jin Kim is currently working in the Applied Quantum Composites Research Center at Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) in Korea. He gained his Ph.D. working in the field of physical chemistry at the Seoul National University in Korea. His research is mainly focused on synthesis, analysis and applications of low dimensional carbon nano materials.

Yelin Oh received her B.S. degree in Chemical System Engineering from Chonnam National University in 2014 and the M.S. degree in Carbon Fusion Engineering from Jeonju University in 2016. Then, she has worked as a research scientist in Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST). Her current research focuses on the synthesis of multi-dimensional nanocarbon materials.

Sang Hyun Lee received the Ph.D. degree from Tohoku University, Japan, in 2008. After that, he joined Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL), the U.S., as a Postdoctoral Researcher in 2009. He is currently the Head of Applied Quantum Composites Research Center in Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) and an Associate Professor at University of Science & Technology (UST). His main research interests include semiconductor nanomaterials and nano-composites for electronic and optoelectronic applications.

Min Park received Ph.D. degree from Seoul National University(SNU), Seoul, Korea, in 1994. He joined University of Michigan Ann Arbor as a Post Doctoral Researcher in 1996. He has been working for Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) for 30 years is now working as a principal researcher of Photo-electronic Hybrid Research Center. His research interests include 2D nanomaterials for soft electronics, microelectronic packaging materials and carbon based polymer composites.

Sungho Lee received his Ph.D. degree in Chemical Engineering from Clemson University in 2007. He is currently a principal research scientist at the Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST), focusing on carbon fibers and their applications.

Sukang Bae received his MS/Ph.D. degree in Sungkyunkwan Advanced Institute Nanoscience and Technology (SAINT) at Sungkyunkwan University, in 2012. Since 2013, he has worked as a senior research scientist in Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST). His primary research focus is in nano-material synthesis (0~2D), fabrication for nano-composite and related functional applications, especially for electronic and opto-electronics.
